ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pasta alla vegetariana; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest parent class. Vegetarian tomato-sauced pasta is a subtype not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta, cooked — used as the primary matrix anchor for this mixed pasta-vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for pasta alla vegetariana as a composite dish. A00LC (cooked pasta) is used as the closest parent matrix anchor; the vegetable and tomato sauce components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to pasta alla vegetariana as a dish entity. SNOMED CT food concepts at this specificity level are not available in the public release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of pasta alla vegetariana is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of cooked pasta tossed with sautéed seasonal vegetables in tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with tomato sauce and vegetables, cooked (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for pasta alla vegetariana as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), and mixed sautéed vegetables (bell pepper, zucchini, carrot, onion) weighted at approximately 55% pasta, 30% vegetables, 15% tomato sauce with olive oil. The input record's per_100g values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 4.5 g, fat 3.75 g, carbohydrate 21.25 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not contain a direct entry for this Italian dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — pasta (white, boiled) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for pasta alla vegetariana as a composite dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white spaghetti/pasta boiled (GI ~45–49, glucose reference). The addition of vegetables, olive oil, and tomato sauce lowers the effective GI relative to plain pasta. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 85.0) / 100 = 38.3, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 21.25 × 4 = 85.0 g. This is a high GL per full serving; portion control is clinically relevant.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 175 mg/100 g; estimated potassium ~230 mg/100 g from vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content at 175 mg/100 g is below the 200 mg/100 g threshold of concern; however, a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 700 mg sodium, so patients with hypertension should request no added salt and choose a smaller portion to keep total sodium within the 1500–2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 21.25 g/100 g; GI ~45 (low); GL per full 400 g serving ~38.3 (high)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the per-100 g glycaemic load is manageable due to the low GI of pasta (~45) and fibre from vegetables, a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 85 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~38, which is high; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request whole-wheat pasta to increase fibre and further lower GI, and pair with a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.75 g/100 g; trans fat 0 g; olive oil as primary fat source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g) and contains no trans fat; olive oil provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols, and the colourful vegetables contribute antioxidants and potassium, making this dish suitable for cardiovascular disease management when consumed in appropriate portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory olive oil MUFAs; nightshade vegetables (tomato, bell pepper)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Olive oil and colourful vegetables provide anti-inflammatory benefits; however, individuals with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should request substitution of bell peppers and tomato sauce with non-nightshade vegetables, as solanine alkaloids in nightshades may aggravate symptoms in a subset of RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta alla Vegetariana is an Italian-origin pasta dish featuring cooked pasta — typically spaghetti or penne — tossed with a medley of sautéed seasonal vegetables including bell peppers, zucchini, carrots, and onions, all simmered in a tangy tomato-based sauce enriched with olive oil and garlic. The dish is fully plant-based (vegan and vegetarian), gluten-containing, and free of dairy and cholesterol. It is widely served in Italian restaurants and casual dining outlets across Singapore. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 3.75 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), and 21.25 g carbohydrate with 2 g dietary fibre and 3 g sugar. Sodium is moderate at 175 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), reflecting the pasta matrix and vegetable fibre content; however, a standard 400 g restaurant serving delivers a high glycaemic load (~38) due to the large carbohydrate volume. Clinically, the dish is suitable for hypertension management given its low saturated fat and moderate sodium, but patients with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control and prefer whole-wheat pasta variants. The olive oil base and antioxidant-rich vegetables make this dish broadly cardioprotective. Nightshade-sensitive individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should note the presence of tomato and bell pepper.
common_user_queries: Is pasta alla vegetariana suitable for diabetics?
Pasta with tomato sauce and vegetables nutrition

confidence_reason: No direct entry for pasta alla vegetariana exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component entries for cooked pasta, tomato sauce, and sautéed mixed vegetables, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white pasta. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), and sautéed mixed vegetables (bell pepper, zucchini, carrot, onion), weighted approximately 55% pasta / 30% vegetables / 15% sauce. No single approved-source entry for pasta alla vegetariana as a composite dish was found. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5) are estimated from USDA olive oil and vegetable fat profiles; potassium_mg (230) is estimated from vegetable content. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish preparation is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based olive-oil preparation with no hydrogenated fats; cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white pasta (boiled); the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to vegetable fibre and olive oil. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting only 'menu_listing' and is not used as a primary nutrient evidence source. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC (cooked pasta) used as closest parent matrix anchor; no composite dish code available. 9. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
